own_identity_reasoning: Localization does not supply its own identity condition. As determined in the Identity analysis, localization is -I (does not carry an identity condition). unity_reasoning: Establishment of localization is a biological process describing the directed movement of substances (cell, substance, or cellular entity) to a specific location. rigidity_reasoning: Motor behavior is anti-rigid because it represents a phase or state of activity that entities engage in temporarily. rigidity_reasoning: Quality is a rigid property because anything that is a quality is necessarily a quality in all possible worlds. identity_reasoning: Quality does not carry an identity condition. Qualities are typically understood as properties or characteristics that inhere in substances (like colors, temperatures, weights). As a non-sortal property, Quality falls into the same category as examples like 'Red' or 'Amount of Matter' which do not provide identity conditions for their instances.
own_identity_reasoning: Quality does not supply its own identity condition. Every instance of quality necessarily requires the existence of some other entity (its bearer) that is not a part or constituent of the quality itself, making it externally dependent according to the formal definition provided.

own_identity_reasoning: Since the Identity analysis determined this entity is -I (does not carry an identity condition), it cannot be +O. For example, the 'speed' of a running process, or the 'efficiency' of a chemical reaction, cannot exist independently - they necessarily require the process they characterize. This satisfies the definition of external dependence: for all instances of process quality, there must necessarily exist an instance of process (which is not a part of the quality itself, but rather what the quality inheres in).

rigidity_reasoning: The property 'obsolete class' is anti-rigid because it is a phase or status that a class acquires at some point in time. There is no meaningful way to distinguish one instance of 'being obsolete' from another instance of 'being obsolete' - obsolescence is a status attribute, not a sortal that provides criteria for individuation and reidentification of its instances. rigidity_reasoning: Thing is the most generic category that encompasses all entities that can possibly exist. If something is a thing (i.e., it exists or can exist), it is necessarily a thing in all possible worlds where it exists. There is no possible world where an entity could cease to be a thing while still maintaining its existence as that entity. An entity cannot 'stop being a thing' and remain the same entity - being a thing is an essential property of anything that exists. Therefore, Thing is rigid (+R).
identity_reasoning: Thing is the most generic, unrestricted property in any ontology - it applies to absolutely everything that exists. It cannot carry an identity condition because it provides no way to distinguish or re-identify its instances. Any identity condition requires some characteristic or relation that holds specifically for instances of that property, but Thing has no such distinguishing characteristics - it merely denotes existence itself. According to the framework, identity conditions can only be supplied by rigid properties with specific characteristics (Definition 6), and Thing is too general to supply any such condition. While specific subsumed properties like Person or Physical Object do carry identity conditions, Thing itself inherits none and supplies none, making it -I.
own_identity_reasoning: Thing does not supply its own identity condition. As the most general category, it is too abstract to provide any specific identity criterion. While Thing might carry identity (+I) if it subsumes properties that supply identity conditions, it cannot itself supply an IC since there is no property subsuming it from which it could inherit an IC, and it lacks the specificity to define its own meaningful identity criterion. Additionally, the Identity analysis determined this entity is -I, which by the constraint '+O implies +I' means it cannot be +O.
unity_reasoning: Thing is the most general category encompassing all entities in an ontology. Its instances can range from integrated wholes (like persons or cars) to scattered collections (like red things) to arbitrary amounts of matter. Since there is no single equivalence relation ω that unifies all instances of Thing - some instances are intrinsic wholes while others are not - Thing does not carry a unity condition. It is -U rather than ~U because it does not exclude instances that are intrinsic wholes; it simply does not require unity across all its instances.
dependence_reasoning: Thing is the most general, abstract category encompassing all entities. It has no external dependence on any other property or entity - instances of Thing do not necessarily require the existence of any other distinct entities to exist. Thing represents maximal independence as the top-level ontological category, with no intrinsic dependencies on more specific properties or external entities.
